summary: Gruber contrasts two company-wide memos from Steve Jobs and Steve Ballmer to illustrate fundamental differences in leadership and strategy between Apple and Microsoft. Jobs's brief MobileMe memo is humble and focused, acknowledging a specific mistake and promising to learn. Ballmer's sprawling strategic outline declares Microsoft will compete everywhere and beat everyone, promising 'absolutely no compromises' — which Gruber argues is delusional. Gruber contends that Ballmer's bravado, particularly his dismissal of Google's search as 'a white page with 10 blue links,' reveals a fundamental inability to recognize what makes competitors successful. The piece argues Microsoft should focus on its core competencies rather than trying to eat every potato on the table.
key_points: Jobs's memo is brief, focused, and credible — acknowledging a specific mistake and promising realistic improvement — while Ballmer's is sprawling bluster promising to dominate every competitor simultaneously

Ballmer's claim of 'absolutely no compromises' ignores that engineering and design are fundamentally about trade-offs, and his mockery of Google's simple search interface shows he doesn't understand why Google succeeds
Despite Microsoft's dominant financials (nearly twice the profit of Apple and Google combined), Ballmer's strategy of competing everywhere signals strategic unfocus
Apple employees may not always agree with Jobs but they believe him, whereas Ballmer's promises 'defy belief' — he's either ignorant or lying, neither of which inspires engineers
Microsoft's real vulnerability is visible in mobile: Windows Mobile, after eight years, barely outsells the one-year-old iPhone, suggesting the 'compete everywhere' strategy is already failing
money_quotes: Jobs's memo is brief, humble, and focused. (That's not to say Jobs is humble, only that the memo is.)
So take my criticism of Ballmer and his style with a few billion dollars worth of salt. But, still.
Engineers, in general, crave facts and detest bullshit.
Ballmer, however, has the demeanor of a successful car salesman. He's so full of bluster that he comes across as being either delusional or full of shit.
"Absolutely no compromises" is not possible. Everything involves compromises.
The old Microsoft could recognize good ideas and copy them; now Microsoft can't even recognize genius.

Gates may be gone, but the attitude is infused in the company's culture. The difference between the '90s and the '00s, though, is that today there are far too many potatoes on the table for any single company, no matter how large, to eat.

Where the memo says 'we will press on to make it a service we are all proud of by the end of this year', in Jobs's Town Hall address to the MobileMe team, it came out more like 'You better fucking fix it by the end of the year'.
key_insight: The contrast between Jobs's focused humility and Ballmer's grandiose bluster reveals that strategic discipline — knowing what not to do — matters more than ambition to do everything.
